request.question: What do the Lost McLeod Mine and the Colomac Mine have in common?

title: Lost Lemon Mine
Lost McLeod Mine
Pebble Mine
Colomac Mine
Colomac Airport
Wheelbarrow Mine
Ryan House and Lost Horse Well
Pitt Lake's lost gold mine
Jolly Jack's Lost Mine
Wopkaimin people
sentences: The Lost Lemon Mine is a legendary lost mine said to be located in the Canadian Province of Alberta.

The story has been retold in countless books with the authoritative version being "The Lost Lemon Mine" by Tom Primrose.The story first appeared in the 1946 edition of the "Alberta Folklore Quarterly" and later in magazines such as "Canada West".
The Lost Lemon Mine has also been featured in television documentary series "Northern Mysteries", and worked into the plot of "The Final Sacrifice".
Countless people have searched for the mine.
A number of searchers have never returned.
Lost McLeod Mine is a legend of a lost mine in the Northwest Territories of Canada.
The story has been featured in many books and magazines.
The events in the legend have led to geographic locations in the Northwest Territories being named Headless Valley and Headless Creek located in Nahanni National Park Reserve.
A creek called Sheep Creek was renamed McCleod Creek in honour of the Mcleod Brothers.
20 people have lost their lives searching for The Lost McLeod Mine.

Pebble Mine is the common name of a mineral exploration project investigating a very large porphyry copper, gold, and molybdenum mineral deposit in the Bristol Bay region of Southwest Alaska, near Lake Iliamna and Lake Clark.
The proposal to mine the ore deposit, using large-scale operations and infrastructure, is controversial.
Proponents argue that the mine will create jobs, provide tax revenue to the state of Alaska, and reduce American dependence on foreign sources of raw materials.

Opponents argue that the mine would adversely affect the entire Bristol Bay watershed; and that the possible consequences to fish populations, when mining effluents escape planned containments, are simply too great of a risk.
Much of this debate concerns the tentative plan to impound large amounts of water, waste rock, and mine tailings behind several earthen dams at the mine site.
The project is on hold after the loss of funding partners in 2013.
The Colomac Mine was a privately owned and operated open pit gold mine located 220 km northwest of Yellowknife in the Northwest Territories in Canada .
The Colomac mine operated between 1990–1992, and 1994–1997.
It was operated by Neptune Resources Limited that had little success in making a profit during its operation.
In 1994, the mine had reopened under Royal Oak Mines Inc.
Both Neptune Resources and Royal Oak Mines where both owned and operated by Peggy Witte.
Due to low gold prices and high cost of mining, Royal Oak Mines was forced into bankruptcy.
The Federal Government of Canada became owners of the mine, along with the related environmental issues.
A major cleanup effort is under way to prevent the mine from polluting the environment, but this might be too late at this stage.
This mine is now owned and controlled by the Indigenous and Northern Affairs department of the Federal government, while Public Works and Government services is the current contracting authority.
Colomac Airport (TC LID: CFY8) , was located near Colomac Mine in the Northwest Territories, Canada where caribou may be found on the runway.
Prior permission was required to land except in the case of an emergency.
The lost Wheelbarrow Mine is claimed to have been located about 10 mi from Potlatch in Latah County, Idaho.
The reported location is 3,871 feet above sea level, at coordinates 46.9975° N, 116.7833° W.
The mine, believed to have been dug prior to 1890, was said to have produced $20,000 in gold, before a falling-out between its operators led to its abandonment.
One of the operators later returned to the area to find the mine again, but was unsuccessful.
In June 1939, miners with the Fitsum Mining company uncovered an abandoned mine matching the description of the Wheelbarrow mine, containing human bone presumed to belong to the other operator.
The Ryan House and Lost Horse Well are historic ruins in Joshua Tree National Park, California, United States.
It was established by the family of J.D. Ryan, the later developers of the Lost Horse Mine, which became the most profitable mine in the area.
The Lost Horse Well at the Ryan Ranch supplied water to the Lost Horse Mine, 3 mi south and 750 ft , by pipeline.

Pitt Lake's Lost Gold Mine is a legendary lost mine said to be near Pitt Lake, British Columbia, Canada, the supposed wealth of which has held the imagination of people worldwide for more than a century.
Ever since the years of the Fraser Canyon Gold Rush prospectors and adventurers have been looking for the mine and gold-rush rumors have evolved into legends repeated and enriched over time.
The mysterious riches are known as Slumach’s Lost Mine, or Lost Creek Mine.
Jolly Jack's Lost Mine is a legend about a hidden placer mine supposed to be located in the Boundary Country of British Columbia.
The mine has been written about by local historians and various magazines.
Many have searched for the lost placer mine of Jolly Jack.
It is a local legend in the Boundary Country.
The Wopkaimin are a small aboriginal tribe that lives in the remote Star Mountains in western Papua New Guinea in what is known as the Fly River socio-ecological region.
The tribe speaks Faiwol.
The Ok Tedi Mine, the third largest open pit copper and gold mine in the world is located in their traditional territory.
Before the coming of the mine with construction starting in 1981 the Wopkaimin lived in a subsistence economy.
The mine severely impacted the tribe, totally disrupting their traditional patterns of life.
For one thing, Tabubil a town of 12,500 to house mine workers was built in the midst of their territory.
Work for wages was available to tribal members, but only at the unskilled level and not on a regular basis.

The Wopkaimin along with many other ethnic groups living in the area now live on a rotating basis between the town of Tabubil, roadside villages along the Kiunga-Tabubil Highway, and in villages away from the mine as jobs are lost or become available.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: What do the Lost McLeod Mine and the Colomac Mine have in common?
hop1_docs: Lost McLeod Mine | Lost McLeod Mine is a legend of a lost mine in the Northwest Territories of Canada.  The story has been featured in many books and magazines.  The events in the legend have led to geographic locations in the Northwest Territories being named Headless Valley and Headless Creek located in Nahanni National Park Reserve.  A creek called Sheep Creek was renamed McCleod Creek in honour of the Mcleod Brothers.  20 people have lost their lives searching for The Lost McLeod Mine.

Colomac Mine | The Colomac Mine was a privately owned and operated open pit gold mine located 220 km northwest of Yellowknife in the Northwest Territories in Canada .  The Colomac mine operated between 1990–1992, and 1994–1997.  It was operated by Neptune Resources Limited that had little success in making a profit during its operation.  In 1994, the mine had reopened under Royal Oak Mines Inc.  Both Neptune Resources and Royal Oak Mines where both owned and operated by Peggy Witte.  Due to low gold prices and high cost of mining, Royal Oak Mines was forced into bankruptcy.  The Federal Government of Canada became owners of the mine, along with the related environmental issues.  A major cleanup effort is under way to prevent the mine from polluting the environment, but this might be too late at this stage.  This mine is now owned and controlled by the Indigenous and Northern Affairs department of the Federal government, while Public Works and Government services is the current contracting authority.

Colomac Airport | Colomac Airport (TC LID: CFY8) , was located near Colomac Mine in the Northwest Territories, Canada where caribou may be found on the runway.  Prior permission was required to land except in the case of an emergency.
I Wonder What They're Doing To-Night (Your Girl and Mine) | I Wonder What They're Doing To-Night (Your Girl and Mine)

List of lost mines | Lost mines are a popular form of lost treasure legend.  The mine involved is usually of a high-value commodity such as gold, silver or diamonds.  Often there is a map (sometimes called a "waybill") purportedly showing the location of the mine.  Common reasons given for the mines being lost include:

Pitt Lake's lost gold mine | Pitt Lake's Lost Gold Mine is a legendary lost mine said to be near Pitt Lake, British Columbia, Canada, the supposed wealth of which has held the imagination of people worldwide for more than a century.  Ever since the years of the Fraser Canyon Gold Rush prospectors and adventurers have been looking for the mine and gold-rush rumors have evolved into legends repeated and enriched over time.  The mysterious riches are known as Slumach’s Lost Mine, or Lost Creek Mine.

Lost Dutch Oven Mine | One of the most famous lost mines in California is known as the Lost Dutch Oven mine.  There are several versions of the story, as is the case with most lost mine legends, perhaps the best known is the one with Tom Schofield, a railroad worker, decided to do some prospecting in his time off.  According to this version, while prospecting in the Clipper Mountains, northwest of Essex (in San Bernardino county), he found an old stone house.  As the house appeared to be long abandoned, he continued on.  Several miles further in his travels he found a spring.  Near the spring he spotted an old trail.  Following the trail, it led him up over a hill where he spotted a huge boulder that was split in two.  The path went through this split boulder, and into what he believed was an ancient Spanish camp.
hop1_titles: I Wonder What They're Doing To-Night (Your Girl and Mine)
response.summary_1: Both passages place the sites in the Northwest Territories, Canada: "Lost McLeod Mine is a legend of a lost mine in the Northwest Territories of Canada" (passage 1) and "The Colomac Mine ... was a privately owned and operated open pit gold mine located 220 km northwest of Yellowknife in the Northwest Territories in Canada" (passage 2). Missing/ambiguous: passage 1 does not say what commodity (e.g., gold) the Lost McLeod Mine involved. Next hop: retrieve sources that state the mineral/commodity associated with the Lost McLeod Mine (or authoritative mentions linking it to gold) to determine whether they share being gold mines beyond their shared Northwest Territories location.
response.hop2_query: Lost McLeod Mine gold Northwest Territories
hop2_docs: Lost McLeod Mine | Lost McLeod Mine is a legend of a lost mine in the Northwest Territories of Canada.  The story has been featured in many books and magazines.  The events in the legend have led to geographic locations in the Northwest Territories being named Headless Valley and Headless Creek located in Nahanni National Park Reserve.  A creek called Sheep Creek was renamed McCleod Creek in honour of the Mcleod Brothers.  20 people have lost their lives searching for The Lost McLeod Mine.

Ruth Mine | The Ruth Mine was a small gold producer about 65 km (40 miles) east of Yellowknife, Northwest Territories.  It operated briefly during 1942 and then again in 1959, extracting only 550 troy ounces (17 kg) of gold thus far.  The old mine is today (2012) significant as one of the last intact abandoned mining properties from the 1940s period in the Northwest Territories, although the site was scheduled to be remediated by the federal government.

Courageous Lake mine | The Courageous Lake mine is one of the largest gold mines in Canada and in the world.  The mine is located in the north of the country in Northwest Territories.  The mine has estimated reserves of 11.4 million oz of gold.

Quartz Mountain mine | The Quartz Mountain mine is one of the largest gold mines in Canada and in the world.  The mine is located in the north of the country in Northwest Territories.  The mine has estimated reserves of 2.74 million oz of gold.

Michael McLeod (politician) | Michael McLeod (born September 6, 1959 in Fort Providence, Northwest Territories) is the current Member of Canadian Parliament representing the Northwest Territories.  He was first elected in 2015 Canadian federal election unseating Dennis Bevington, who was the former New Democratic Party MP for the riding.  McLeod was a former member of the Legislative Assembly of the Northwest Territories, Canada, as well as the former mayor of Fort Providence.

Robert C. McLeod | Robert C. McLeod (born March 3, 1960 in Aklavik, Northwest Territories, Canada) is carpenter and private contractor as well as a current territorial level politician and current member of the Northwest Territories Legislature.

Bob McLeod (politician) | Robert R. McLeod (born 1952) is a Canadian politician.  He is currently a member of the Legislative Assembly of the Northwest Territories the 12th Premier of the Northwest Territories, having held that position since October 26, 2011.
response.summary_2: Both sites are located in the Northwest Territories of Canada. Passage 1 explicitly describes the Lost McLeod Mine as a legend of a lost mine in the Northwest Territories (passage 1); the retrieved passages do not identify the commodity for Lost McLeod, so a shared "gold mine" status is unconfirmed.
response.answer: They are both located in the Northwest Territories of Canada.
